title: Inplace fused (leaky)relu+(leaky)dropout for memory savings

root_cause.mechanism: Feature request — Inductor does fuse relu+dropout into a single kernel, but does not produce fully inplace/allocation-less code. The backward pass requires saving either the input or the relu mask, preventing full elimination of intermediate buffers. The high watermark of memory is determined by saved-for-backward tensors, not intermediate allocations.
root_cause.trigger: User wanting maximum memory savings from relu+dropout fusion, especially for memory-constrained GPUs
root_cause.confirmed_by: experimentation — Chillee showed AOT_FX_GRAPHS output demonstrating fusion saves seed instead of full mask; engineer_20 explained high watermark is dominated by saved-for-backward

diagnostic_path.result: Chillee showed Inductor already fuses into single kernel saving only seed + relu mask

diagnostic_path.action: Asked if fully allocation-less is possible
diagnostic_path.result: Chillee explained backward needs input or mask; for fp32 mask is cheaper, for fp16 about equal
diagnostic_path.conclusion: Fundamental autograd constraint prevents full inplace

diagnostic_path.result: Chillee explained this would require saving output + reading it back, more expensive than mask

diagnostic_path.action: Benchmarked manual inplace impl vs torch.compile
diagnostic_path.result: torch.compile gives equal or better performance
diagnostic_path.conclusion: Inductor fusion already near-optimal for speed

diagnostic_path.result: Inplace intermediates don't help with peak memory, which is dominated by activations saved for backward
diagnostic_path.conclusion: Memory saving from inplace intermediates is minimal

resolution.description: Inductor already fuses these ops into single kernels. Full inplace is prevented by autograd's need to save values for backward. The memory saving from inplace intermediates is minimal because peak memory is dominated by saved-for-backward activations, not intermediate buffers.

new_diagnostic_tools: AOT_FX_GRAPHS=1 — environment variable to inspect AOT Autograd's forward/backward graph decomposition

phase2.confidence: medium — extensive discussion but issue still open, user continues proposing alternatives

title: [TorchInductor] Add support for Pascal GPUs (P100, GTX 1080, etc)

root_cause.mechanism: TorchInductor/Triton required Volta+ GPUs (SM70+). Pascal GPUs (SM60/61) lacked fp16 matmul support in Triton (tl.dot), some atomic operations, and had shared memory limitations. The has_triton check explicitly excluded pre-Volta GPUs.
root_cause.trigger: Users with Pascal GPUs (P100, GTX 1080) attempting to use torch.compile/TorchInductor
root_cause.confirmed_by: code reading — has_triton check at torchinductor/utils.py required Volta+; shintaro-iwasaki ran Triton tests on P100 (278 failed, 3983 passed)

diagnostic_path.action: ptillet outlined what Pascal support would need
diagnostic_path.result: Identified 4 requirements: disable fp16, use aten for matmul/conv, fix Triton assert, test

diagnostic_path.action: shintaro-iwasaki ran Triton tests on P100
diagnostic_path.conclusion: Most of Triton already works on Pascal

diagnostic_path.result: Tests were running Triton directly, not through Inductor
tl.dot broken, some atomics unsupported, shared memory issues
cas-based atomics not as bad as feared in microbenchmarks
Inductor version didn't use atomics, found DCE bug (missing return)

resolution.description: Pascal GPU support was incrementally added through Triton patches and Inductor changes. Multiple PRs across Triton and PyTorch repos addressed the gaps.
workaround_configs: torch._inductor.config.triton.cudagraphs = False — needed for some Pascal testing

new_configs: torch._inductor.config.triton.cudagraphs = False — disable CUDA graphs for GPU compatibility testing
new_symptoms: GPU architecture compatibility gate — has_triton check silently excludes older GPUs without clear error message
new_workarounds: Use aten backend for matmul/conv on pre-Volta GPUs
Disable fp16 on Pascal GPUs

phase2.confidence: medium — issue is a feature request with incremental progress, not a single clear resolution

title: dynamo: handle contiguous graph breaks

root_cause.mechanism: Feature request — when Dynamo encounters contiguous graph breaks (multiple unsupported instructions in sequence), it creates a separate resume/trace boundary for each one. This is wasteful when the instructions don't contain torch ops.
root_cause.trigger: Models with many consecutive unsupported ops (e.g., numpy code), causing many graph breaks in a row
root_cause.confirmed_by: code reading — existing optimization at convert_frame.py:360 partially addresses this but not fully

diagnostic_path.action: Proposed optimization for contiguous graph breaks
diagnostic_path.result: engineer_14 agreed it's a good idea, numpy code is the main case
Partial implementation exists but doesn't fully optimize
Dynamo team won't work on it for months

resolution.description: Valid optimization proposal for reducing overhead from contiguous graph breaks, especially in models with numpy code. Marked low priority by Dynamo team.

title: torch.compile() accuracy minifier breaks when using dynamic shapes

root_cause.mechanism: Inductor with dynamic shapes produced NaN values after ~5 iterations on T5-style models, while static shapes and aot_eager backend produced correct results. The accuracy minifier itself couldn't handle symbolic shapes, crashing before it could isolate the bug. The NaN appeared to originate in attention-related custom kernels under dynamic shapes.
root_cause.trigger: torch.compile with dynamic shapes on HuggingFace T5-style model with custom architecture, specifically during multi-iteration token generation
root_cause.confirmed_by: experimentation — aot_eager produces correct results (isolating to Inductor); static shapes produce correct results (isolating to dynamic shape handling); NaNs appear after iteration 5 consistently

diagnostic_path.action: Ran accuracy minifier on dynamic shapes model
diagnostic_path.result: Minifier itself crashed — doesn't support symbolic shapes

diagnostic_path.action: Tested aot_eager backend with dynamic shapes
diagnostic_path.conclusion: Bug is in Inductor, not Dynamo or AOT
Bug specific to dynamic shapes in Inductor

diagnostic_path.result: Error jumps from 1e-4 to ~300 after iteration 5, then NaNs
diagnostic_path.conclusion: Not gradual drift — sudden catastrophic failure

diagnostic_path.result: Same crash as minifier — split reduction fails with dynamic shapes
diagnostic_path.conclusion: Dynamic shape split reduction is broken

diagnostic_path.action: Tried debugging with graph break via print
diagnostic_path.result: Got separate error: __getattr__ not supported in graph mode

diagnostic_path.action: Created standalone repro with custom T5 model

diagnostic_path.result: No NaNs but same error magnitude jump pattern
diagnostic_path.conclusion: NaN is hardware-dependent but error pattern is universal

resolution.description: Fixed via improvements to Inductor's dynamic shape handling. The minifier was also separately fixed to handle symbolic shapes. Chillee worked on minifier fix.
workaround_configs: torch._dynamo.config.assume_static_by_default = True — use static shapes as workaround
torch._dynamo.mark_dynamic(x, 0) — explicitly mark dynamic dims (but may hit split reduction bug)

new_configs: torch._dynamo.config.assume_static_by_default — treat all shapes as static unless explicitly marked dynamic
new_symptoms: accuracy degrades suddenly after N iterations (not gradually) — jumps from 1e-4 to ~300 then NaN
accuracy minifier crashes with dynamic shapes — NotImplementedError in split reduction
new_workarounds: Use assume_static_by_default=True to avoid dynamic shape code paths
Test with aot_eager backend to isolate Inductor-specific issues
new_diagnostic_tools: Multi-iteration accuracy tracking — run N iterations and plot error over time to detect delayed correctness failures
assume_static_by_default + mark_dynamic — selective dynamic shape testing to narrow down which dimension causes issues

title: [compile] output does not match eager mode

root_cause.mechanism: group_norm decomposition in AOT Autograd produced numerically divergent results compared to eager mode. The decomposed group_norm accumulated floating point errors that, when combined with unstable model behavior (random weights near decision boundaries), caused wildly different classification outputs.
root_cause.trigger: Using torch.compile with functorch (make_functional_with_buffers + vmap + grad) on resnet18 with randomly initialized weights and GroupNorm layers
root_cause.confirmed_by: minifier + experimentation — engineer_17 used accuracy minifier to isolate divergence to a less-than comparison, zou3519 compared float32 vs float64 and showed eager float32 vs float64 already diverge significantly (0.16 abs, 20 rel), proving model instability not compiler bug

diagnostic_path.conclusion: Second case is just floating point precision, not a bug

diagnostic_path.conclusion: Small perturbation flipping a comparison, not systematic error

diagnostic_path.action: engineer_17 ran minifier overnight restricting to float divergence only
diagnostic_path.result: Got repro with RMSE 0.00017 on group_norm-related code
diagnostic_path.conclusion: Some real numerical divergence exists in decomps

diagnostic_path.result: No accuracy issue with pretrained weights
diagnostic_path.conclusion: Random weights create unstable decision boundaries
Bug is in decompositions, not Inductor

diagnostic_path.action: kshitij12345 identified group_norm decomposition as root cause
zou3519 did comprehensive float32 vs float64 comparison
diagnostic_path.result: eager float32 vs float64 already has 0.16 abs diff, model is numerically unstable
diagnostic_path.conclusion: Not a compiler bug — model with random weights is inherently unstable

diagnostic_path.result: Opened separate issue for segfault found during investigation
diagnostic_path.conclusion: Original issue is expected numerical behavior

resolution.description: Divergence traced to numerical instability in randomly-initialized resnet18, not a compiler bug. Float32 eager vs float64 eager already diverge significantly. Pretrained weights show no issue. Separate segfault bug filed.

new_symptoms: numerical instability masquerading as correctness bug — random weights + decision boundaries cause 100% element mismatch that looks like compiler error but is model instability
new_workarounds: Use pretrained weights to verify correctness instead of random initialization
Compare compile(float64) vs eager(float64) to rule out model instability

float32 vs float64 comparison to distinguish model instability from compiler bugs

title: torch.compile doesnt respect use_deterministic_algorithms during the backward()

root_cause.mechanism: AOT Autograd traces the backward graph during forward compilation, running meta implementations of backward ops ahead-of-time. If use_deterministic_algorithms is set after compilation but before backward(), the compiled backward won't respect it because the graph was already traced with the old setting.
root_cause.trigger: Setting use_deterministic_algorithms(True) after torch.compile but before backward() — especially for ops like ReflectionPad2d that have non-deterministic backward implementations
root_cause.confirmed_by: code reading — bdhirsh identified the AOT ahead-of-time tracing as the mechanism; albanD and engineer_17 discussed design solutions

diagnostic_path.action: Reported that compiled backward ignores deterministic mode
diagnostic_path.result: No error raised despite non-deterministic op in backward
diagnostic_path.conclusion: Compiled backward doesn't check runtime deterministic flag

diagnostic_path.result: Backward graph traced during forward, uses compile-time settings
Could guard on deterministic flag like grad_mode
Two needed fixes: warning on mismatch + guard to invalidate

diagnostic_path.action: albanD questioned whether mixed determinism is useful
diagnostic_path.result: Agreed: just error on mismatch, don't support dynamic switching
No longer reproduces — likely fixed by backward tracing changes

resolution.description: Issue resolved by architectural changes to backward tracing in later PyTorch versions. The original problem was that AOT Autograd traced backward during forward compilation, ignoring runtime deterministic flag changes.

new_symptoms: compiled backward ignores runtime use_deterministic_algorithms — AOT ahead-of-time tracing captures compile-time settings
new_workarounds: Set use_deterministic_algorithms before torch.compile, not after — ensures backward graph is traced with correct flag

phase2.confidence: high — clear root cause, verified fix

extraction_quality_notes: Good diagnostic depth despite being an architectural design discussion. High ontology value — novel failure mode (compiled backward ignoring runtime flags).

title: AttributeError: module 'z3' has no attribute 'ExprRef'

root_cause.mechanism: Python package name collision — user installed 'z3' package which shadows the 'z3-solver' (Microsoft Z3) that PyTorch's symbolic shape reasoning depends on. torch._dynamo imports z3 expecting z3-solver but gets the wrong package.

resolution.description: Package name collision between pip 'z3' and 'z3-solver'. User needs to uninstall 'z3' package. Also noted: torch.compile not officially supported on Windows at the time.

new_symptoms: Python package name collision — wrong z3 module shadows z3-solver dependency
new_workarounds: Uninstall conflicting 'z3' pip package when z3-solver is needed

title: failed while executing int_truediv

root_cause.mechanism: Warning spam from sympy interpreter: int_truediv between float tensors (MetaProxy types) raises NotImplementedError which is caught and triggers a warning log. The warning is harmless but extremely noisy in logs. Root cause is that the sympy interpreter's associative op handler doesn't gracefully handle type mismatches between symbolic int division and float tensor operands.
root_cause.trigger: torch.compile on models that involve integer division operations with tensor operands — common in transformer architectures with dynamic shapes
root_cause.confirmed_by: code reading — engineer_17 identified the exception path in torch/utils/_sympy/interp.py:159 where NotImplementedError is caught and logged as warning

diagnostic_path.action: User reported spammy warning messages appearing in logs
diagnostic_path.result: Thousands of 'failed while executing int_truediv' warnings per compilation

diagnostic_path.action: engineer_17 analyzed the exception handling code
diagnostic_path.result: NotImplementedError in associative op handler caught and logged as warning
diagnostic_path.conclusion: Warning is cosmetic, not a correctness issue

diagnostic_path.action: engineer_15_gh tried to write minimal repro
diagnostic_path.result: Surprisingly hard — couldn't trigger the exact path with simple scripts
diagnostic_path.conclusion: Requires specific graph structure from real models

diagnostic_path.action: engineer_15_gh found repro via existing test case

resolution.description: Fixed the noisy warning in sympy interpreter's int_truediv handler. The warning was harmless (correctness unaffected) but created massive log spam. Fix suppressed the NotImplementedError warning for expected type mismatches.

new_symptoms: massive warning log spam from sympy interpreter — 'failed while executing int_truediv' appears thousands of times per compilation

title: [torch.export] Cannot export TorchVision fasterrcnn_mobilenet_v3_large_fpn

root_cause.mechanism: Detection models (FasterRCNN, MaskRCNN, FCOS, etc.) use data-dependent control flow extensively — bounding box filtering based on scores, NMS with variable output size, ROI operations with dynamic box counts. These patterns are fundamentally incompatible with torch.export's static graph requirement. The data-dependent expressions (DDEs) originate in user code (torchvision), not in the framework.
root_cause.trigger: torch.export.export_for_training on any torchvision detection model (FasterRCNN, MaskRCNN, KeypointRCNN, FCOS, RetinaNet, SSD, SSDLITE)
root_cause.confirmed_by: code reading — pianpwk traced DDEs to torchvision/ops/boxes.py:97 (_batched_nms_coordinate_trick) and torchvision/models/detection/roi_heads.py

diagnostic_path.conclusion: Strict mode can't handle attribute mutation

diagnostic_path.result: Different error — data-dependent shape from symbolic_shapes

diagnostic_path.conclusion: Draft export works as temporary solution

diagnostic_path.result: Fixed one error but 10+ more DDEs remain
diagnostic_path.conclusion: Too many data-dependent patterns to fix one by one

diagnostic_path.action: Tested all detection models on latest nightly
diagnostic_path.result: All fail with various DDE errors
diagnostic_path.conclusion: Systemic issue across all detection models

diagnostic_path.result: DDEs from user code (torchvision), not framework
diagnostic_path.conclusion: Fix needs to go upstream in torchvision

diagnostic_path.action: laithsakka confirmed his DDE improvements don't help user code
diagnostic_path.conclusion: Need torchvision model rewrites for export compatibility

resolution.description: Fundamental incompatibility between detection model architectures (data-dependent control flow for NMS, ROI operations) and torch.export's static graph requirement. Fix requires upstream torchvision model rewrites. draft_export works as partial workaround with specializations.
workaround_configs: torch.export.export_for_training(model, args, strict=False) — avoids attribute mutation error

new_symptoms: data-dependent dynamic shapes (DDEs) from user model code — cascade of export failures from NMS, ROI operations, bounding box filtering
draft_export succeeds but with specializations — may not generalize
new_workarounds: Use draft_export=True for detection models (produces specialized graph)
Rewrite data-dependent control flow with torch.cond (requires extensive model changes)
new_diagnostic_tools: draft_export to identify which specializations export makes — shows what's incompatible

phase2.confidence: high — systematic testing across all detection models, clear architectural incompatibility

title: [Export AOTI] dynamic_shapes export and compile degraded output

root_cause.mechanism: torch.export with dynamic_shapes produces an exported program with incorrect guards that bake in static assumptions from the dummy input shape. Specifically, interpolate_pos_encoding creates a statically-shaped tensor (4097 elements based on pos_embed), and the export system generates a guard Eq(((s0//14))*((s1//14)) + 1, 4097) that constrains dynamic dims. When inference uses different shapes, this guard either fails (torch.compile) or produces silently degraded output (AOTI).
root_cause.trigger: Using torch.export with dynamic_shapes (Dim) for models containing position encoding with static parameters (e.g., DepthAnything/Video-Depth-Anything)
root_cause.confirmed_by: code reading — pianpwk traced the incorrect guard in the exported program

diagnostic_path.action: Exported same model with dynamic W,H using Dim
Tested with export dummy input matching inference shape
diagnostic_path.conclusion: Bug only when inference shape differs from export shape
torch.compile surfaces error that AOTI silently masks

diagnostic_path.action: Provided minimal repro with DepthAnything model

diagnostic_path.conclusion: Static pos_embed shape leaks into dynamic guard

resolution.description: Root cause identified: position encoding with static parameters creates incorrect guards in exported program. AOTI silently produces wrong output while torch.compile raises error. No fix landed yet.

new_symptoms: AOTI silently masks export guard violations — produces degraded output instead of raising error
export guard baking in static parameter shapes as dynamic constraints
new_workarounds: Use export dummy input with same shape as inference input (defeats dynamic shapes purpose)
Use fixed shapes instead of Dim for models with position encoding
new_diagnostic_tools: Compare AOTI vs torch.compile on exported program — torch.compile surfaces errors AOTI hides
Inspect exported program guards for static shape leakage

phase2.confidence: high — clear root cause identified, minimal repro available

title: [AOTI] Severe Performance Regression with FP16 Autocast in AOTInductor for Small Batch Sizes

root_cause.mechanism: Linear decomposition's bmm-vs-mm folding heuristic fails for fp16 with small batch sizes. When batch_first=True, transpose makes activations non-contiguous, and the heuristic chooses bmm over mm (to avoid a copy). But cuBLAS selects a terrible fp16 bmm kernel (ampere_fp16_s16816gemm) that is 30x slower than the fp32 equivalent. Additionally, max_autotune can't help because '64-bit indexing is not yet implemented for triton templates', so no Triton backend is available.
root_cause.trigger: MultiHeadAttention with batch_first=True, fp16 autocast, small batch size (bs=1-2), on Ampere GPUs
root_cause.confirmed_by: experimentation — yushangdi traced slow kernel to aoti_torch_cuda_bmm_out, profiled cuBLAS kernel selection, showed contiguity determines mm-vs-bmm choice

diagnostic_path.action: Profiled fp16 vs fp32 AOTI inference
diagnostic_path.result: fp16 10x slower at bs=1, 2.7x at bs=2
diagnostic_path.conclusion: Severe regression specific to fp16 small batches

diagnostic_path.result: ampere_fp16_s16816gemm kernel from aoti_torch_cuda_bmm_out taking 51ms vs 1.5ms fp32
diagnostic_path.conclusion: cuBLAS picks bad fp16 bmm kernel

diagnostic_path.action: Investigated why bmm instead of mm
diagnostic_path.result: batch_first=True causes transpose, non-contiguous input, heuristic avoids copy by using bmm
diagnostic_path.conclusion: Folding heuristic makes wrong choice for fp16

diagnostic_path.action: Discovered fp32 perf was accidentally good
diagnostic_path.result: fp32 only fast because requires_grad=True triggers different code path; with requires_grad=False, fp32 also slow
Still falls back to aten bmm; Triton templates lack 64-bit indexing
diagnostic_path.conclusion: No alternative kernel available via max_autotune

diagnostic_path.result: Manual transpose + contiguous + linear gives correct fast performance

resolution.description: Root cause identified as bmm-vs-mm folding heuristic not accounting for fp16 cuBLAS kernel quality. engineer_20 proposed fix: transpose activations inside linear to make contiguous, fold to mm, transpose output back. PR for heuristic change would be accepted.
workaround_configs: Use batch_first=False and manually transpose input (make contiguous)
TORCHINDUCTOR_MAX_AUTOTUNE=1 — does NOT help (Triton templates lack 64-bit indexing)

new_configs: TORCHINDUCTOR_MAX_AUTOTUNE=1 — try all kernel options including Triton templates (blocked for this case by 64-bit indexing gap)
new_symptoms: fp16 autocast performance regression at small batch sizes — cuBLAS selects poor bmm kernel for non-contiguous fp16 tensors
requires_grad=True accidentally triggers different (faster) code path — misleading benchmarks
new_workarounds: Use batch_first=False with manual transpose+contiguous to force mm instead of bmm
Disable requires_grad to get realistic inference benchmark (requires_grad=True triggers different path)
new_diagnostic_tools: Profile individual cuBLAS kernel selection to identify poor algorithm choices
Check tensor contiguity to understand mm-vs-bmm heuristic decisions

phase2.confidence: high — thorough investigation by yushangdi and engineer_20, clear root cause

title: [compile] Correctness and reproducibility issue

root_cause.mechanism: Inductor fused a KV cache roll/rotate operation as a pointwise kernel, but roll is not a pointwise operation — it's a data movement pattern. The fused kernel produced incorrect results for the sliding window cache rotation used in Gemma-2 style models with HybridCache.
root_cause.trigger: HuggingFace transformers generate() with torch.compile on models using sliding window attention with HybridCache (Gemma-2-2b), specifically when cache rotation uses tensor indexing with roll
root_cause.confirmed_by: experimentation — v0i0 identified the incorrect fusion of roll as pointwise; engineer_21_gh reproduced on H100/A100; issue was intermittent (non-deterministic)

diagnostic_path.action: Noticed generation output differs between compiled and non-compiled model
diagnostic_path.conclusion: Correctness issue with torch.compile on transformers generate

diagnostic_path.action: Tested scalar vs tensor slicing in cache update
diagnostic_path.result: Scalar slicing works, tensor slicing with roll fails
diagnostic_path.conclusion: Issue related to roll/rotate operation in cache

diagnostic_path.action: zou3519 asked to try mode=reduce_overhead and backend=eager/aot_eager

diagnostic_path.result: Confirmed different results on consecutive runs

diagnostic_path.action: v0i0 identified incorrect pointwise fusion of roll
diagnostic_path.result: Roll fused as pointwise but it's a data movement op
diagnostic_path.conclusion: Root cause found — incorrect fusion pattern

resolution.description: Fixed by preventing Inductor from incorrectly fusing roll operations as pointwise kernels. PR submitted by v0i0 to address the fusion logic.

new_symptoms: non-deterministic correctness failure — compiled model sometimes produces correct output, sometimes wrong
KV cache corruption from incorrect fusion of data movement operations (roll/rotate)
new_workarounds: Use scalar indexing instead of tensor indexing with roll in cache update code
new_diagnostic_tools: Compare scalar vs tensor slicing to isolate fusion-related correctness issues
Run multiple iterations to detect intermittent correctness failures

phase2.confidence: high — clear root cause identified and fix in progress

title: Voxtral + Export/AOTI

root_cause.mechanism: Custom SDPA op's meta implementation returned wrong tensor layout — used empty_like(query) which inherits non-contiguous strides from query, but the real kernel returns contiguous output. This caused AOTI to generate code assuming wrong memory layout.
root_cause.trigger: Exporting Voxtral model with custom_sdpa op through ExecutorTorch/AOTI
root_cause.confirmed_by: code reading — yushangdi identified the meta implementation at executorch custom_ops.py:154 returning non-contiguous tensor

diagnostic_path.action: Exported Voxtral model, ran with AOTI
yushangdi traced to custom_sdpa meta implementation

resolution.description: Fixed custom_sdpa meta implementation to return contiguous tensor (empty_like(query).contiguous() instead of empty_like(query)). The non-contiguous meta output caused AOTI to generate code with wrong memory layout assumptions.

new_symptoms: meta kernel layout mismatch — meta implementation returns different stride/contiguity than real kernel, causing AOTI codegen errors

phase2.confidence: high — clear root cause, minimal fix

extraction_quality_notes: Short conversation (6 comments) but high ontology value — meta kernel layout mismatch is a novel failure mode.

title: torch.compile gives 'AssertionError: Deduped args out of bounds' for working pytorch code

root_cause.mechanism: When calling the user compiler with fake_example_inputs(), the fake inputs didn't work correctly with the eager/aot_eager backends. The 'deduped args out of bounds' assertion in AOT Autograd fired because input deduplication logic produced invalid indices during tracing of models using einops operations (rearrange/einsum with complex reshape patterns).
root_cause.trigger: torch.compile on model using einops.rearrange + einsum with VectorQuantization (complex reshape + einsum patterns in training mode)
root_cause.confirmed_by: engineer_17 reproduced, confirmed error persists with backend='aot_eager', minifier produced different error, traced to fake_example_inputs handling

diagnostic_path.result: Error confirmed on both CPU and CUDA

diagnostic_path.conclusion: CPU inductor backend crash — separate issue

diagnostic_path.result: Same deduped args error, minifier script writable
diagnostic_path.conclusion: Issue is in AOT Autograd input dedup, not inductor
Multiple failure modes from same root cause

diagnostic_path.action: engineer_02 patched to use real example_inputs instead of fake
diagnostic_path.conclusion: Root cause was fake_example_inputs not working with some backends

resolution.description: Fixed on master — patched example input handling in output_graph.py

configs.description: Run minifier at dynamo stage to isolate tracing issues

diagnostic_tools.name: Minifier with aot_eager backend

title: [Regression][PT2.1][Dynamic] torch.add torch._dynamo.exc.TorchRuntimeError with out= tensor

root_cause.mechanism: PT2.1 improved dynamism so that scalar arguments like alpha become dynamic by default. The torch.add out= lowering path never supported dynamic alpha, causing TorchRuntimeError when dynamic=True.
root_cause.trigger: torch.compile(dynamic=True) on torch.add(input, other, alpha=scalar, out=tensor)
root_cause.confirmed_by: engineer_17 confirmed — same root cause affects torch.addr and multiple other ops with out= + alpha pattern

diagnostic_path.action: User reported regression from PT2.0 to PT2.1
diagnostic_path.result: torch.add with out= and alpha fails with dynamic=True
alpha is now dynamic, out= path doesn't support dynamic alpha
diagnostic_path.conclusion: Lowering path incomplete for dynamic scalars

diagnostic_path.action: User reported same issue in torch.addr and other ops
diagnostic_path.conclusion: Systemic gap in out= lowering paths for dynamic scalars

resolution.description: Use torch._dynamo.mark_dynamic to manually mark dimensions dynamic and remove dynamic=True as workaround. Fix: add dynamic alpha support to out= lowering paths.

symptoms.description: Operations that worked with static shapes fail with dynamic=True when scalar arguments become dynamic

workarounds.description: Use torch._dynamo.mark_dynamic() for specific dimensions instead of global dynamic=True to avoid dynamic scalar issues

phase2.confidence: high
phase2.extraction_notes: Clear regression pattern. Reveals systemic gap: out= tensor paths don't handle dynamic scalars. 7 related issues suggest fuzzer-discovered pattern.

title: Custom op not compatible with compile mode='reduce-overhead' and linear layers for large inputs

root_cause.mechanism: Custom CUDA kernel did not set the CUDA stream via at::cuda::getCurrentCUDAStream(). When using mode='reduce-overhead' (CUDA graphs), the kernel ran on the wrong stream, producing zeros for elements beyond the initial chunk.
root_cause.trigger: torch.compile(mode='reduce-overhead') with nn.Linear before custom CUDA op, large input sizes
root_cause.confirmed_by: User (vlejd) debugged: first N elements correct then zeros, adding cudaDeviceSynchronize before kernel fixed first run. Root cause: missing stream in kernel launch.

diagnostic_path.result: Compiled model output is all zeros

diagnostic_path.action: User investigated: first N elements correct, rest zeros

diagnostic_path.result: Fixed first run but broke second run
Tutorial code was wrong, not PyTorch
diagnostic_path.conclusion: User error in custom op CUDA kernel

resolution.description: Fix: set CUDA stream in custom kernel launch. PR submitted to pytorch/extension-cpp tutorial.

symptoms.description: Compiled output has first N elements correct then zeros — indicates CUDA stream synchronization issue in custom ops

workarounds.description: Custom CUDA kernels must call at::cuda::getCurrentCUDAStream() when used with mode='reduce-overhead' (CUDA graphs)

phase2.confidence: high
phase2.extraction_notes: Excellent diagnostic progression. The partial-zeros pattern is a strong diagnostic signal for stream issues. User self-diagnosed with systematic ablation.

title: vmap x compile silently incorrect

root_cause.mechanism: Custom op's register_fake kernel returned tensor with incorrect strides compared to the real implementation. The fake vquantile used index_select+squeeze giving strides (2,1) while torch.quantile returns strides (1,3). Inductor generates code based on fake kernel metadata, so stride mismatch caused silent incorrectness.
root_cause.trigger: torch.compile(torch.vmap(custom_op)) where custom op's fake kernel has wrong output strides
root_cause.confirmed_by: guilhermeleobas confirmed not reproducible with backend='aot_eager', traced stride mismatch between fake and real kernel outputs

diagnostic_path.action: Fixed fake kernel to match real strides
diagnostic_path.conclusion: User error in custom op registration

resolution.description: Fix fake kernel to produce matching strides. PyTorch added assert_size_stride check in inductor (#152353) and better error message for fake kernel mismatch (#143765).

symptoms.description: Silent incorrectness when custom op's register_fake returns tensors with different strides than the real implementation

diagnostic_tools.name: aot_eager backend for vmap+compile
diagnostic_tools.description: Use backend='aot_eager' to isolate whether silent incorrectness is from Inductor codegen vs tracing

phase2.confidence: high
phase2.extraction_notes: Important pattern: custom op fake kernel metadata must exactly match real kernel. Led to two defensive PRs adding stride assertions.

title: torch.compile Error: RuntimeError: aten::_conj() Expected Tensor but found complex

root_cause.mechanism: When tracing backward through torch.mul(tensor, 1j), the autograd derivative calls other.conj() which dispatches to aten._conj(). During tracing, the scalar-tensor (1j) gets converted back to a Python complex scalar, but _conj expects a Tensor. The python arg parser converts 1j to tensor for forward, but backward trace doesn't maintain this conversion.
root_cause.trigger: torch.compile on model using complex number multiplication (torch.mul(x, 1j)) with requires_grad=True
root_cause.confirmed_by: bdhirsh created minimal repro: torch.compile(backend='aot_eager')(lambda x: torch.mul(x, 1j)) with complex64 input

diagnostic_path.conclusion: AOT Autograd backward tracing issue, not inductor

diagnostic_path.result: Identified problematic op in backward graph

diagnostic_path.conclusion: Scalar-tensor roundtrip breaks in backward trace

diagnostic_path.action: engineer_17 linked to AudioLM and broader complex number issues
diagnostic_path.conclusion: Systemic gap in complex number support

resolution.description: Complex number support gap. Workaround: rewrite code to avoid complex literals (Euler's identity), but this can cause 10x slowdowns (msaroufim benchmarked rotary embeddings).

symptoms.description: RuntimeError on aten::_conj when compiling models that multiply tensors by complex scalars (1j) with autograd

diagnostic_tools.name: TORCH_LOGS=aot_graphs
diagnostic_tools.description: Dump AOT Autograd forward/backward graphs to identify problematic ops in backward trace

phase2.confidence: high
phase2.extraction_notes: Great diagnostic chain from user→aot_eager isolation→minimal repro. Reveals systemic complex number gap. Performance impact of workaround (10x) is critical context.

title: Silent incorrectness between static torch.compile vs eager

root_cause.mechanism: When compiling expand+mutation patterns, Inductor infers tensor alignment on first run. On second run with unaligned input, Inductor clones the input to satisfy alignment assumptions, but this clone causes mutations (a[-1] = 3) to be lost — they apply to the clone, not the original. expand() creates a tensor with stride 0, making mutation semantics subtle (writes to all elements in eager).
root_cause.trigger: torch.compile(dynamic=False) on code that does x.expand(n) followed by in-place mutation (a[-1] = 3)
root_cause.confirmed_by: engineer_02 traced: first run gets aligned tensor, second run gets unaligned, clone for alignment loses mutation

diagnostic_path.result: expand() creates stride-0 tensor, so a[-1]=3 writes to ALL elements in eager

diagnostic_path.conclusion: Fake tensor metadata doesn't capture stride-0

diagnostic_path.result: First run aligned → guard set, second run unaligned → clone → mutation lost
diagnostic_path.conclusion: Alignment inference on mutated views is unsafe

resolution.description: Proposed fixes: (1) never infer alignment on mutated inputs, (2) add post-inductor epilogue to copy back clones, (3) guard on alignment for mutated inputs

symptoms.description: Silent incorrectness where in-place mutations are lost because Inductor clones input for alignment, disconnecting mutation from original tensor

configs.name: torch._functorch.config.fake_tensor_crossref
configs.description: Cross-reference fake tensor metadata against concrete tensors to detect stride/shape mismatches ('all' mode)

phase2.confidence: high
phase2.extraction_notes: Exceptional diagnostic depth. Reveals fundamental tension between alignment optimization and mutation semantics. The fake_tensor_crossref tool is a powerful debugging technique.

title: sympy.core.relational.StrictLessThan throws Invalid NaN comparison on VLLM model

root_cause.mechanism: ValueRanges.floordiv doesn't correctly preserve type when 0 is in divisor range. When one argument has integer range [0, int_oo] and other has float range [-oo, oo], the product 0 * oo produces sympy.nan, which then causes Invalid NaN comparison in StrictLessThan.
root_cause.trigger: Compiling VLLM model (bigscience/bloom-560m) that produces value ranges with integer/float type mixing in floordiv
root_cause.confirmed_by: engineer_17 traced: floordiv with 0 in b returns ValueRanges.unknown() but products include NaN from 0*oo before the check

diagnostic_path.action: User reported error on VLLM model

diagnostic_path.result: Reproduced with specific shape environment setup

diagnostic_path.action: engineer_17 traced to floordiv with mixed int/float ranges

diagnostic_path.result: Difficult because shape_env shifts ranges to positive before evaluation
diagnostic_path.conclusion: Bug only triggers through specific code paths

resolution.description: Fix needed in ValueRanges.floordiv to handle NaN products from mixed int/float range arithmetic

symptoms.description: Invalid NaN comparison error from sympy when ValueRanges arithmetic produces NaN through mixed integer/float range operations

phase2.confidence: high
phase2.extraction_notes: Deep sympy/ValueRanges diagnostic. Hard to reproduce synthetically because shape_env normalizes ranges. Affects VLLM models specifically.

title: massive number of runtime asserts can hamper compile times

root_cause.mechanism: When a model produces thousands of unbacked SymInts (e.g. from split operations), runtime asserts accumulate with expressions containing hundreds of symbols. Printing/sorting these expressions has quadratic behavior (~15 seconds per assert log line), and constructing inequality checks (expect_true → le) is 20% of compile time.
root_cause.trigger: Models with many split/slice operations producing thousands of unbacked symbols, each generating runtime asserts with sum of all symbols
root_cause.confirmed_by: Logs show ~15 seconds between each runtime assert line, TTFB profile shows expect_true=33%, le construction=20%, slice_forward=37%

diagnostic_path.result: Logs show ~4000 lines of runtime asserts with ~15s between each
diagnostic_path.conclusion: Quadratic behavior in runtime assert processing

diagnostic_path.action: User avoided regression by avoiding split calls
diagnostic_path.conclusion: Workaround: restructure model to avoid many splits

resolution.description: User workaround: avoid split operations. Potential fix: sym_sum to aggregate symbols, optimize le construction, address quadratic print sorting.

symptoms.description: Compile time grows quadratically with number of runtime asserts when model produces many unbacked SymInts from split/slice operations

workarounds.description: Restructure model to avoid many split/slice operations that produce thousands of unbacked SymInts causing quadratic compile time

phase2.confidence: high
phase2.extraction_notes: Performance pathology in symbolic shapes. Quadratic behavior from sympy expression sorting. Related to #140835.

title: Compiled nn.Module with tensor subclass can't be moved to another device

root_cause.mechanism: During compilation, Dynamo takes weakrefs to module parameters in multiple places: (1) MetaConverter.tensor_meta dict in FakeTensorMode, (2) HASATTR guards in variables/builder.py, (3) TENSOR_MATCH guard code in guards.py. After compilation, these weakrefs persist. When Module._apply() calls swap_tensors() to move params to another device, swap_tensors refuses because outstanding weakrefs exist.
root_cause.trigger: compiled_module.cpu() or compiled_module.to(device) after torch.compile() on module with tensor subclass parameters
root_cause.confirmed_by: bdhirsh traced 3 weakref sources; azahed98 monkey-patched weakref to get creation tracebacks; removing one source reduced count from 3 to 2

diagnostic_path.action: Identified error in swap_tensors due to outstanding weakrefs
diagnostic_path.result: RuntimeError: cannot swap tensors with outstanding weakrefs
diagnostic_path.conclusion: Weakrefs from compilation prevent device movement

diagnostic_path.action: bdhirsh traced weakref sources in compile pipeline
diagnostic_path.result: Found 3 sources: MetaConverter, HASATTR guard, TENSOR_MATCH guard
diagnostic_path.conclusion: Multiple places stash weakrefs to params

diagnostic_path.action: engineer_17 suggested clearing MetaConverter weakrefs at end of compilation
diagnostic_path.result: Feasible — already breaks refcycles at compile end
diagnostic_path.conclusion: Some weakrefs can be cleaned up

diagnostic_path.result: HASATTR guard weakref is incidental not fundamental — can be removed
diagnostic_path.conclusion: Guards don't need weakrefs to tensors

diagnostic_path.action: azahed98 fixed one source, tracked remaining

resolution.description: WIP: Systematically remove weakrefs to parameters after compilation. One source fixed, remaining tracked. Critical for torchao quantization + model offloading workflows.

symptoms.description: RuntimeError in swap_tensors when moving compiled module to another device due to outstanding weakrefs from compilation

diagnostic_tools.name: weakref creation tracing
diagnostic_tools.description: Monkey-patch weakref module to capture tracebacks at weakref creation time, identifying which compilation steps create persistent weakrefs

phase2.confidence: high
phase2.extraction_notes: Exceptionally rich 17-comment diagnostic thread. Critical for torchao+diffusers workflow (quantize→offload→compile). Multiple engineers systematically traced weakref sources. Still open — in-progress fix.

title: PyTorch 2.0: AssertionError fake_mode is not None (possibly because of einops.rearrange)

root_cause.mechanism: DDPOptimizer.compile_fn asserts fake_mode is not None, but fake_mode_from_tensors() returns None when example inputs don't contain fake tensors. This happens with DDP + certain model patterns (einops.rearrange). The fix: create a new FakeTensorMode() as fallback instead of asserting.
root_cause.trigger: torch.compile with DDP (DistributedDataParallel) on model using einops.rearrange
root_cause.confirmed_by: engineer_17 provided one-line patch: replace assert with fallback FakeTensorMode() creation

diagnostic_path.action: User reported error with DDP + einops.rearrange
diagnostic_path.result: AssertionError: fake_mode is not None in distributed.py
diagnostic_path.conclusion: DDP compilation path assumes fake inputs always present
Not einops-specific — model structure triggers it

diagnostic_path.result: Create FakeTensorMode() when None instead of asserting
diagnostic_path.conclusion: Simple fallback fix, no perf impact

resolution.description: One-line fix in DDPOptimizer: replace assert with FakeTensorMode() fallback. Fixed on master, available in next nightly.

phase2.confidence: high
phase2.extraction_notes: Simple fix but important DDP interaction pattern. User had follow-on issues (separate thread #90550). Minifier usability gap noted — user didn't know how to use it.
